title: Soy Sauce on Linen
stainChemistry: Soy sauce contains caramel color compounds (melanoidins), salt, amino acids, and tannin-like phenolics from fermentation. On linen, the melanoidins bond to fiber through a combination of hydrogen bonding and Maillard-type crosslinks. The high salt content acts as a mordant, fixing the brown color deeper into the fiber matrix.
whyThisWorks: Flushing with cold water immediately removes salt and loose pigment from linen. The tannin spotter breaks melanoidin-fiber bonds. An oxidizer addresses the persistent brown color. Pre-rinsing is unusually important for soy sauce — the salt component acts as a dye fixative, so removing it quickly prevents the stain from becoming permanent.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply Tannin Formula (acidic tannin spotter) and tamp gently. Work from edge to center — 5-10 min dwell time
2. Apply H2O2 6% (professional oxidizer) to break down color-causing bonds. Monitor every 5 min — 5-15 min dwell time
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) and leveling agent to remove broken-down tannin — 2-3 min dwell time
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min dwell time
warnings: NEVER use ammonia or alkaline products on tannin stains — permanently darkens the color

steps: 1. Blot immediately with a clean white cloth to absorb as much liquid as possible — never rub
2. Mix 1 tbsp Dawn dish soap with 1 cup hydrogen peroxide 3%. Apply to stain — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat until color fades
4. Rinse with cold water to remove all cleaning solution
5. Air dry — do NOT use dryer until stain is completely gone
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 applications, or if you see any color change in the fabric. Take to a professional dry cleaner
warnings: Always test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — linen dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
NEVER use baking soda, ammonia, or alkaline cleaners on tannin stains — they permanently darken the color
Avoid heat until stain is fully removed

neverDo: No strong concentrated acids — weakens cellulose
No chlorine bleach on colored linen
fiberSensitivities: Strongest natural fiber when wet — very durable
Wrinkles easily but tolerates aggressive cleaning
Neutralize acid spotters after use on cellulose

escalation.when: If soy sauce stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is soy sauce on linen, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Has it been exposed to any alkaline products (baking soda, ammonia)?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.

materialWarnings: ALWAYS test on a hidden seam or inside hem first — linen dyes may not tolerate spotting agents.
